original_abstract:
Radioimmunotherapy (RIT) is a compelling strategy for cancer treatment, yet its efficacy is often hampered by the poor physical penetration of large antibodies into tumors and their prolonged circulation times. While smaller vehicles like antibody fragments and peptides offer highly specific targeting and rapid clearance, they typically suffer from high renal retention. Nanobodies—the smallest functional antigen-binding fragments derived from camelid heavy-chain-only antibodies—face similar challenges. 

In this study, we demonstrate that kidney accumulation of nanobodies is primarily governed by the polarity of the C-terminal amino acid tag. We engineered three versions of the anti-HER2 nanobody 2Rs15d with varying C-termini: Myc-His-tagged, His-tagged, and untagged. Dynamic imaging in Wistar rats using $^{111}$In-DTPA-labeled constructs showed that removing the C-terminal tags reduced kidney accumulation by 70% compared to the Myc-His variant. Furthermore, co-infusing untagged nanobodies with Gelofusin, a clinical plasma expander, achieved a total reduction of 90%. Parallel experiments in HER2-positive tumor-bearing mice using $^{177}$Lu-DTPA-2Rs15d confirmed these findings, showing a 95% reduction in renal uptake without compromising tumor-targeting capacity.

Based on these results, we established a generic framework for minimizing the renal burden of radiolabeled nanobodies. Dosimetric analysis of the optimized $^{177}$Lu-labeled untagged nanobody (with Gelofusin) revealed a balanced radiation dose of 0.90 Gy/MBq to both the tumor and kidneys, with negligible exposure to healthy tissues. In a head-to-head comparison with $^{177}$Lu-DTPA-Trastuzumab, the nanobody delivered significantly less radiation to the tumor (6-fold lower) but reduced the radioactive burden to the blood, lungs, liver, and spleen by factors ranging from 34 to over 4,000.

Most significantly, weekly treatment with $^{177}$Lu-labeled anti-HER2 nanobodies in mice with established HER2-positive tumors resulted in near-complete growth blockade and a dramatic increase in event-free survival ($P < 0.0001$). Histological evaluation confirmed the absence of renal inflammation or necrosis. These findings underscore the critical role of C-terminal composition in nanobody design and highlight the potential of these tracers as a potent adjuvant therapy for treating metastatic and minimal residual disease.
humanized_abstract:
Radioimmunotherapy (RIT) is considered to be a promising approach to cancer therapy. Antibodies are often retained within the tumor but have limited penetration due to their size. Additionally, large molecules have longer circulation times which can negatively impact their efficacy as cancer therapeutics. Peptides and antibody fragments provide for a more targeted delivery to the tumor and exhibit a shorter half-life thereby reducing non-target interactions. Unfortunately, these small molecules are rapidly excreted by the kidneys. Consequently, they accumulate to a greater extent in renal tissue, thus limiting their tumour-killing effect. Nanobodies which are the smallest antigen-binding fragments derived from camelid heavy-chain-only antibodies are no exception to this.

Kidney uptake of nanobodies is shown to be mainly determined by the charge of the amino acid sequence at the C-terminus of the construct. Specifically, three versions of the anti-HER2 nanobody 2Rs15d with different C-terminal modifications were investigated: Myc-His tagged, His tagged and untagged. While dynamic scintigraphy using $^{111}$In-DTPA linked to the nanobodies provided insight in the in vivo behavior of the molecules, significant reduction of renal uptake of 70% compared to Myc-His tagged 2Rs15d was observed in all animals for the untagged construct. When untagged 2Rs15d was coinjected with Gelofusin, the plasma expander used in clinical treatment of radioactive contamination by expanding plasma volume, renal uptake was decreased by 90%. In addition, the reduction of renal uptake of $^{177}$Lu-DTPA-2Rs15d by 95% in mice bearing HER2 overexpressing tumors had no effect on tumor accumulation.

Based on these data, we developed a model to design a therapy that minimizes the renal burden of radiolabeled nanobodies. The dosimetry of our optimized $^{177}$Lu-labeled, UT, nanobody (with Gelofusin, GF) indicated that the total radiation dose to the tumor and kidneys was 0.90 Gy/MBq, while the radiation doses to other organs and tissues were minimal. A comparison with $^{177}$Lu-DTPA-Trastuzumab revealed that the nanobody had 6-fold less tumor radiation and 34 to over 4,000-fold less radiation in blood, lungs, liver and spleen.

Weekly administration of 177Lu-labeled anti-HER2 nanobodies to mice bearing established tumors grew the tumors to a minimal extent and significantly (P < 0.0001) increased event-free survival. The kidneys were free of inflammation or necrosis as determined by histology. These results provide strong evidence for the importance of the C-terminus modification on nanobody function and indicate that the tracers have great potential as an adjuvant therapy in the treatment of metastatic and minimal residual disease.
